segment-text: EXT. MAYCOMB, ALABAMA - DAYBREAK

We are looking down on the small Southern town of Maycomb, Alabama. It is just before dawn and in the half light, we can see cotton farms, pine woods, the hills surrounding Maycomb, and the Courthouse Square of Maycomb itself. The CAMERA STARTS TO SLOWLY MOVE down to the Square. As we begin to see the stores and the offices which comprise the town, a young woman's voice is heard OFF CAMERA:
In 1932 this was the world I knew. It wasn't a very big world, but neither was I. (a beat) I was six years old.
THE CAMERA STARTS TO SLOWLY MOVE DOWN the main residential street leading away from the Square. JEAN LOUISE (VOICE OVER)

During this THE CAMERA HAS COME TO REST ON the Finch house and yard. The Finch house is a small frame house, built high off the ground and with a porch in the manner of Southern cottages of its day. The yard is a large one, filled with oaks, and it all has an air of mystery about it in the early morning light.
What I remember was that I was to begin school in two weeks, for the first time. What I didn't know was that my whole world was soon to change.

WALTER CUNNINGHAM, a thin, raw-boned farmer in his late fifties, comes into view. He is carrying a crocker sack full of hickory nuts. He passes under the oak tree at the side of the house as a young girl, six, dressed in blue jeans, drops from one of its branches to the ground. She brushes herself off and goes toward Mr. Cunningham.
Why, good morning, Mr. Cunningham. My daddy is getting dressed. Would you like me to call him?
No, ma'm. I wouldn't care to bother -SCOUT (eagerly) Why, it's no bother, Mr.
Cunningham. He'll be happy to see
MED. SHOT - WALTER CUNNINGHAM
He seems ill at ease and embarrassed. ATTICUS, Scout's father, comes OUT of the kitchen door.
Her's Mr. Cunningham.
Good morning, Walter.
Good morning, Mr. Finch. I didn't want to bother you none. I brought these here as part of my entailment.
Well, I thank you. The collards we had last week were delicious.
Atticus picks up the sack. These are hickory nuts.
And these hickory nuts look mighty nice.
Mr. Cunningham mutters something and starts on. CLOSE SHOT - ATTICUS, SCOUT
Scout, I think next time Mr. Cunningham comes, you'd better not call me.
Well, I thought you'd want to thank him.
The ANGLE WIDENS as Atticus starts for the front yard to get the morning paper, Scout after him.
Well, I do. But I think it
embarrasses him to be thanked.
Why does he bring you all this stuff?
(explaining patiently) He's paying me for some legal work I did for him.
(curious) Why does he pay you like that?
Because that's the only way he can. He has no money. Is he poor?
Atticus picks up the paper and opens it.
SCOUT
We are, indeed.
Are we as poor as the Cunninghams?
Not exactly. The Cunninghams are country folks, farmers, and the crash hit them the hardest.
CAMERA PULLS BACK to INCLUDE Calpurnia, in her late fifties at the screen door, calling:
Breakfast, Scout. Call your brother.
Calpurnia GOES back INSIDE.
(matter of factly) Atticus, Jem is up in that tree yonder. (points toward the oak) He says he won't come down until you agree to play football for the Methodists.
Atticus walks toward the tree. In a tree-house, high in the tree, sits JEM. He is 10, with a serious, manly little face. Presently, he is scowling.
(very calmly) Son, why don't you come on down and have your breakfast now. Calpurnia has a good one. Hot biscuits, and...
No, sir. Not until you agree to play football for the Methodists.
(wryly) Son, I can't do that. I explained to you I'm too old to get out there. After all, I'm the only father you have and you wouldn't want me to go out there and get my head knocked off, would you?
(interrupting with great indignation) Can you imagine me telling them when they ask me where my Papa is that he's too old to be here playing football? I'm not going to come down. Suit yourself.
Atticus starts for the kitchen door. Scout has been watching them. She follows her father into the kitchen.
INT. KITCHEN

It is a large, old-fashioned country kitchen. This is Calpurnia's domain, and she runs it with absolute authority. There is a large kitchen range, a sink, a table and chairs for breakfast, and more informal family meals. Since Scout's and Jem's mother is dead, while Atticus is at work, Calpurnia has charge of the children, and the kitchen has become a retreat and headquarters for them. Atticus and Scout come into the kitchen.
Poor thing. Maybe I'd better take him a little something up there.
(sternly) You tend to your own marbles.
Scout glumly begins to eat her breakfast. up the paper to read.
EXT. TREE HOUSE - MED. SHOT

Jem is eating a biscuit. Scout comes climbing up the tree. She holds in one hand a napkin with some bacon. She hands it up to him, and starts down again. He leans over the edge of the tree house, calling down to her.
Scout, bring me a doughnut. All right.
She continues on down.
And some football magazines. All right.
She continues on.
And some blankets.

CAMERAL PULLS BACK TO FULL SHOT As Scout reaches the ground and see MISS MAUDIE ATKINSON across the street in her yard, working on her flowers. She is a strong, warm-hearted woman, keenly interested in Atticus and the children
(yelling over) Good morning, Miss Maudie-MAUDIE What's going on over there?
(pausing long enough to explain) I'm having a terrible time, Miss Maudie. I'm about to wear myself out. Jem is staying up in that
tree until Atticus says he'll play
(YELLING) He's too old for anything. Every time I want him to do something, he's to old.
(YELLING) He can do plenty of things.
Atticus comes out of the kitchen door, dressed for work. He seems unconcerned at all the commotion.
You be good, children, and mind Cal.
He starts on.
Good morning, Maudie.
Good morning, Atticus.
He continues on out of the yard.
(YELLING over to Maudie as his father leaves the yard) He won't let me have a gun. (MORE)
He'll only play touch football with me...never tackle.
(YELLING back) He can make somebody's will so airtight, you can't break it. You count your blessings and stop complaining, both of you. Just thank your stars he has the sense to act his age.
She walks away. MED. SHOT - SCOUT, JEM
(calling up to Jem) Jem, he is pretty old.
(still firm) I can't help that.

He swings down to the lower limb in disgust and looks down into MISS STEPHANIE CRAWFORD'S collard patch next door. A boy is sitting among the collards. Sitting down he is not much higher than the collards. It is DILL.
has solemn, owlish face, a knowledge and imagination too old for his years. He looks up at Jem.
(tentatively) Hey -Hey, yourself.
I'm Charles Baker Harris. read So what?
I thought you'd like to know I can read. You got anything needs reading? I can do it.
How old are you? Four and a half?
Going on seven.
Shoot, no wonder then. Scout yonder's been reading since she was born and she don't start school till next month. You look right puny for going on seven.
I'm little, but I'm old.
Dill climbs through the fence.
Folks call me Dill.
Jem climbs down the tree to get a closer look. CLOSER SHOT - DILL

I'm from Meridian, Mississippi. I'm spending two weeks next door with my Aunt Stephanie. My mama worked for a photographer in Meridian. She entered my picture in a Beautiful Child Contest and won five dollars. She gave the money to me and I went to the picture show twenty times on it.
GROUP SHOT - SCOUT, DILL, JEM
Our mama's dead. We got a daddy. Where's your daddy
I haven't got one. Is he dead? No.
Well, if he's not dead, you've got one, haven't you?
Dill blushes.
(he has decided to take Dill under his wing Hush, Scout.
CAMERA PULLS BACK TO FULL SHOT As Calpurnia comes out the kitchen door. to them. She walks over
Dill, this is Calpurnia.
Please to know you, Dill.
I'm pleased to know you. My daddy owns the L and N Railroad. He's going to let me run the engine all the way to New Orleans.
(impressed) Is that so?

He says I can invite -MR. RADLEY, in his seventies, a regal, austere man, walks by. Scout and Jem see him and become very subdued, as if they were afraid. Their attention leaves Dill and he senses this and looks at them to see what is happening.
There goes the meanest man that ever took a breath of life.
Calpurnia goes back into the kitchen. CLOSE SHOT - DILL, JEM
(his curiosity aroused) Why is he the meanest man?
Well, for one thing, he has a boy named Boo that he keeps chained to a bed in that house over yonder.
MOVING SHOT - SIDEWALK - JEM, DILL, SCOUT
As they start to move out of the yard, Scout follows behind them. They go down the sidewalk past Miss Stephanie's house.
Boo only comes out at night when we are asleep and it's pitch dark. When you wake up at night you can
hear him. He walks like this.
screen door, but he was gone time Atticus got there.
REVERSE ANGLE - EXT. RADLEY HOUSE

They are standing by a light pole now, staring at the Radley house and yard. The house is low, and was once white, with a deep front porch and green shutters. But it was darkened long ago to the color of the slate grey yard around it. Rain-spotted shingles droop over the eaves of the veranda. Oak trees keep the sun away. The remains of a picket fence drunkenly guards the front yard. A "swept" yard that is never swept, where Johnson grass and Rabbit Tobacco grass grow in abundance. Dill's eyes have widened. intrigued. He is becoming truly
Is that why my Aunt locks up so tight at night? Sure.
Wonder what he does in there? Looks like he'd just stick his head out the door. What would you do if he stuck his head out the door now?
(with real terror I'd die... that's what I'd do.
CLOSE TWO SHOT - DILL, JEM
Wonder what he looks like?
Well, judging from his tracks, he's about six and a half feet
tall. He eats raw squirrels and
a long, jagged scar running across his face. His teeth are yellow and rotten. His eyes popped. And he drools most of the time.
Aw, I don't believe you.

ANGLE WIDENS TO INCLUDE Miss Stephanie Crawford, Dill's aunt. She is in her late fifties. She is a spinster and the neighbourhood gossip. She comes up behind them without them hearing her. She has habit of half shouting when she talks.
STEPHANIE
They jump and Dill looks as if he is about to faint.
(turning pale) My Lord, Aunt Stephanie, you almost gave me a heart attack.
Dill, I don't want yo playing around that house over there. There's a maniac lives there and he's dangerous.
(to Dill You see. (to Stephanie I was just trying to warn him about Boo, and he wouldn't believe me.
Well, you'd better believe him, Mr. Dill Harris.
Tell him about the time Boo tried to kill his papa.
Well, I was standing in my yard one day when his mama come running out and yelling, "He's killing us all."Turned Out Boo was sitting in the living room cutting up the paper for his scrapbook and when
his daddy come by, he reached over
His eyes popping with excitement.

They wanted to send Boo to the asylum, but his daddy said no Radley was going to any asylum, so they locked him up in the basement of the Courthouse till he almost died of the damp and then his daddy brought him back home. And there he is to this day, sittin' in there with his scissors, lord knows what he's doin' or thinkin'. Maudie calls to her
Hello, Stephanie.
Hello, Maudie (starts toward Miss
Now children, run over and play in your own yard.
Dill starts across the street followed by Jem and Scout. They stare at the Radley house as they GO. Suddenly Jem stops in the middle of the street, calling to the others. Look.
He points toward the Radley house. Dill and Scout stop.
Didn't you see him? He was just there at the window.
He points his finger toward the Radley house, Dill and Scout look and as they do, Jem raises his hand.
And watch out- he's liable to come
running out with his scissors and
He pretends he is stabbing them. and run OFF. Scout and Dill SCREAM
EXT. FINCH YARD - MOVING SHOT - SCOUT, JEM, DILL

It's almost five o'clock in the afternoon. They are playing Follow-the-Leader. Jem is leading. He, at this moment, is turning hand-springs and Scout is following. Dill is half-attempting to follow but is not able to execute them. Jem then runs for the tree house, climbs up, stand for a beat and starts down as Scout starts to tackle the tree. Jem runs for the front sidewalk, Scout behind him, Dill behind her, having avoided climbing the tree. In the distance, we HEAR the town clock strike five.
It's five o'clock.
He starts to run out of the yard.
Where you going?
Time to meet Atticus.
She runs after Jem; Dill follows after her.
Why do you call your daddy Atticus?
'Cause Jem does.
Why does he?
I don't know. He just started to when he first began talking.
EXT. SIDEWALK - MOVING SHOT
They run up the street, south, towards town. down. Jem slows

Mrs. Dubose is on her porch. (to Dill) Listen, no matter what she says to you, don't answer her back because she has a Confederate pistol in her lap under her shawl and she'll kill you quick as look at you.
They walk cautiously on and start to pass the Dubose house. It is an old and run-down house. It has steep
front steps and a dog-trot hall. MRS. HENRY LAFAYETTE
DUBOSE sits on the front porch in her wheel chair. Beside her is a Negro girl who takes care of her, JESSIE.
Hey, Mrs. Dubose.
CLOSE TWO SHOT - MRS. DUBOSE, JESSIE MRS. DUBOSE
Don't you say "hey" to me, you ugly girl. You say "good afternoon, Mrs. Dubose".
Scout is walking slowly on past Mrs. Dubose.

You come over here when I'm talking to you. It's too bad your mama never lived to raise you properly. Lord knows what your daddy teaches you. Why don't they put a dress on you. Can't your daddy afford to buy you a dress?
CAMERAL PULLS BACK TO MED.SHOT Scout, Jem and Dill keep on going. uncomfortable by her. They are made very
(SCREAMING Listen to me when I'm talking to you. Don't your daddy teach you to respect old people? And you, young lady...
As she freezes as if she knows what's coming and dreads
...Jean Louise Finch.
The children see Atticus coming down the sidewalk and run
Atticus, this is Dill. He's Miss Stephanie's nephew. Hello, Dill.
Mrs. Dubose sees Atticus now, too and begins SCREAMING. Atticus Finch!
MRS. DUBOSE
ANGLE ON ATTICUS As he bows gracefully to her.
Good afternoon, Mrs. Dubose.
The children are trying to hide behind Atticus as he passes her house. They begin to GIGGLE nervously at each other.
You look like a picture this afternoon.
(whispering He don't say a picture of what.
My goodness gracious, look at your
(pointing to the camellia bushes) (MORE)
Did you ever see anything more beautiful? Mrs. Dubose, the gardens at Bellingrath have nothing to compare with your flowers.
Oh, I don't think they're as nice
as they were last year.
I can't agree with you. I think your yard is going to be the show place of the town.
(whispering to Dill behind Atticus He gets her interested in something nice so she forgets to be mean
(lifting his hat again Grand seeing you, Mrs. Dubose.
They move on down the street.
CLOSE SHOT - MRS. DUBOSE, JESSIE
Mrs. Dubose had a look of senile contentment on her face.
EXT. SIDEWALK AND YARD OUTSIDE MISS MAUDIE'S
Miss Maudie is still working in her yard. The children run IN followed by Atticus. He crosses over to Miss Maudie. The children run into their yard. TWO SHOT - MISS MAUDIE, ATTICUS
Her's your baking soda, Maudie.
Thank you, I hated to bother you but I have to make a cake tonight and I completely forgot it when I ordered my groceries today.
He starts away.
Very hot today wasn't it?
Yes, indeed it was. Atticus...
Atticus starts toward her.
Stephanie called me awhile ago. She was telling me something about Tom Robinson and the Ewell girl. Have you heard anything about it?

Yes. About four this afternoon Bob Ewell came running into town looking for Sheriff Tate. He claimed Tom had raped and beaten his oldest girl, Mayella. Heck went out to Robinson's and he denied doing it, but Heck brought him into jail.
I've never heard of Tom in trouble before, have you? No.
Calpurnia calls from the Finch yard. Supper.
Well, I'm sorry to hear about it. So was I.
A pause.
They look at each other.
Thank you again for the groceries,
Atticus.
Good night, Maudie. Good night.
MISS MAUDIE'S POINT OF VIEW
She watches Atticus and the children start for their house. Dill watches them go and then starts to Miss Maudie. BACK TO MISS MAUDIE Dill comes up to her.
You live all alone don't you? Yes.
Aunt Stephanie told me all about your husband gambling and running up all those debts and deserting you and breaking your heart and then dying. I was so sorry to hear it.

Well, I think Stephanie has exaggerated just a little bit, son. My heart's not broken at all, and as to being poor, it doesn't seem to me like I'm any poorer than anybody else around here. But thank you for your sympathy anyway. STEPHANIE (O.S.) (calling) Dill-I'm coming.
He starts for his house.
(calling after him Good night. Good night.
He runs across the street as Miss Maudie starts for her house.
INT. SCOUT'S ROOM - MED.SHOT - ATTICUS, SCOUT
She is undressed and in bed. Atticus is seated on the bed. Scout is reading to him from "Robinson Crusoe."

(reading "What I missed most was a companion. I had two cats which I brought ashore on my first raft, and I had a dog..." (closes the book Atticus, do you think Boo Radley ever comes and looks in my window? Jem says he does. He said this afternoon when we were over by their house -ATTICUS (sharply Scout, I told you and Jem to leave those poor people alone. I want you to stay away from there and stop tormenting them. (meekly Yes, sir.
She opens the book to read. Atticus takes his watch out and looks at it.
I think we'd better not read any more tonight, honey. It's late.
What time is it?
Can I see your watch?
He gives it to her.
As she reads the inscription.
"To Atticus, my beloved husband." Jem says this watch is going to be his some day. That's right. Why?
Well, it's customary for the boy to have his father's watch.
What are you going to give me?
Well, I'm afraid I haven't much else of value that belongs to me. But there's a pearl necklace and a ring that belonged to your mother which I've put away and they're to be yours.
Scout smiles. Atticus kisses her cheek. watch and goes toward Jem's room.
Good night, Scout. Good night.
INT. JEM'S ROOM
Jem, too, is in bed.
Good night, Jem. Good night.
Atticus GOES OUT.
INT. SCOUT'S ROOM - CLOSE SHOT - SCOUT
She lies in bed thinking.
JEM (O.S.)
He takes his
How old was I when Mama died? Two.
How old were you? Six.
Old as I am now? Yes.
Was Mama pretty? Yes.
EXT. FRONT PORCH - CLOSE SHOT - ATTICUS
He has come back to the porch. He rocks in his chair. From the porch he can hear the children's questions. Was Mama nice?
(as if he'd answered these questions a million times) Yes -SCOUT (O.S.) Did you love her? Yes.
Did I love her? Yes.
Do you miss her? Yes.
JEM (O.S.)
There is silence. Atticus rocks. He listens to the night SOUNDS. ANGLE WIDENS as JUDGE TAYLOR, seventy-five, COMES UP on the porch. JUDGE Hello, Atticus.
Hello, Judge. Kind of warm tonight, isn't it?
He takes a handkerchief and wipes his face.
How is Mrs. Taylor?
She's fine, thank you.
Judge Taylor sits in a chair next to Atticus.
INT. JEM'S BEDROOM - CLOSE SHOT
Jem is in bed, listening.
Atticus, you heard about Tom
Yes, sir.
Grande jury will get around to charging him tomorrow. I thought I'd let you now it's your case.
EXT. PORCH - JUDGE TAYLOR AND ATTICUS
Yes, sir. (pause) Well, thanks for the business, Judge.
Thank you. I'll send a boy over for you tomorrow when his case comes up. Yes, sir.
ANGLE WIDENS as Judge Taylor gets up.
I'll see you tomorrow. Yes, sir. And thank you. Yes, sir.
JUDGE ATTICUS
Judge Taylor leaves. Again there is silence. Atticus rocks. He listens to the night's SOUNDS. Jem comes TO the porch.
Can I sit with you for a while?
All right, son.
Jem COMES outside. He gets in his father's lap. His father holds him, rocking.
MED. SHOT - ATTICUS, JEM
Scout asleep?
Yessir. Did you work hard today?
I worked pretty hard. That little boy that's come to stay with Stephanie seems like a nice boy. Yessir.
Son, you know that man who works in Mr. Link Deas' yard, Tom
Yes, sir.
Well, he's in trouble, serious trouble. Judge Taylor asked me to take the case, and I --
He looks down and sees the boy is asleep. at his son as we He smiles down
INT. KITCHEN - EARLY NEXT MORNING - MED. SHOT
Calpurnia is at the sink. Dill COMES IN. Good morning. Scout and Jem are eating.
Good morning. You're up mighty bright and early.
I've been up since four.
(incredulously

(with great nonchalance Oh, yes. I always get up at four. It's in my blood. You see, my daddy was a railroad man until he got rich, and now he flies aeroplanes. One of these days he's just going to swoop down here at Maycomb and pick me up and take me for a ride.
Have you had breakfast? Oh, yes.
Calpurnia GOES OUT of the kitchen.
Dill moves closer to
(whispering Everything was quiet when I looked over at the Radley house this morning. (looking at Jem Did you hear Boo out last night? No.
Jem gets up and walks OUT of the kitchen. BEHIND him. Dill and Scout

Jem COMES INTO
the yard.
Dill and Scout come up to him.
I bet you a "Grey Ghost" against two "Tom Swifts", Jem, and you won't go any farther than Boo Radley's gate.
A pause. Dill looks at Jem slyly.
You're scared to, ain't you?
(stalling I'm not scared. I go past Boo
Radley's house nearly every day of
Always running.
(furious at her You hush up, Scout.
Folks in Meridian County aren't as scared as folks in Maycomb County.
(scornfully I'll tell you, you're wasting your breath, Dill. He's scared and I don't blame him.
ANGLE WIDENS as Scout gets tire and slaps it back to them.
Let's roll in the tire. All right.
Jem takes hold of the tire and Scout gets inside of it. When she is inside, Jem suddenly pushes it with all his might.
That'll teach you to call me scared.
He pushes the tire down the sidewalk with all the force of his body. It leaves the sidewalk, goes across the
gravel road to the sidewalk in front of the Radley place,

through the gate, up the Radley sidewalk, hits the steps of the porch and rolls over on its side. Dill and Jem watch this with helpless terror. When the tire has stopped, Scout, dizzy and nauseated, and unaware of where she is, lies on the ground.
(YELLING frantically Scout. Scout. Get away from there. Scout, come on.
EXT. RADLEY YARD
Scout raises her head and sees where she is. frozen with terror. She is
Come on, Scout. Don't just lie there. Get up, can't you?
Scout gets to her feet. She is trembling with fear.
EXT. SIDEWALK ACROSS THE STREET - DILL, JEM
(terrified Go get her, Jem.

MOVING SHOT Jem rushes in through the gate, gets his sister by the hand, then looks up at the house, drops her hand, runs up the steps to the front door, touches it, comes running down, grabs the tire, takes his sister by the hand and starts running out of the yard.
Run for your life, Scout.

And they run out of the yard, up the sidewalk to their own yard. Dill runs fast behind them. When they get to the safety of their yard, they are all exhausted and fall on the ground. But Jem is slated by his feat of touching the Radley house. GROUP SHOT - SCOUT, JEM, DILL
Now, who's a coward? You tell them about this back in Meridian County, Mr. Dill Harris.
Dill looks at Jem with new respect.

(eagerly I'll tell you what let's do. Let's go downtown to the courthouse and see that room they locked Boo up in. My aunt says it's bat infested, and he almost died from the mildew. Come on. I bet they got chains down there and instruments of torture. He starts OUT of the yard.
Scout and Jem reluctantly
THE COURTHOUS SQUARE - MED. SHOT

A group of FOUR IDLERS sit lounging under some live oak trees. They watch with eagle eye whatever happens on the Square and in the courthouse. Dill, followed by Scout and Jem, come by them. One of the men, HIRAM TOWNSEND, recognizes Scout and Jem. He is in his seventies and is dressed in work clothes.
If you're looking for your daddy, he's inside the courthouse.
Thank you, but we're not lookin'
for -Jem gives her a yank and a look and she shuts up and they

They go toward the courthouse.
What's your daddy doin' up there?
He's a lawyer and he has a case. The grand jury is charging his client today. I heard somethin' about it last night when Judge
Taylor came over.
Let's go watch. Oh, no, Dill. that.
He wouldn't like
Dill GOES IN the courthous, FOLLOWED by Scout and Jem. Scout and Jem seem worried about following but reluctantly decide to.
INT. COURTHOUSE LOWER FLOOR
This is the floor where all the county offices are. three children COME IN. They look around. The
Where's your daddy?
He'll be in the courtroom. Up there.
He points toward the stairs. Dill, Scout, and Jem solemnly climb the stairs to the second floor.
INT. COURTHOUSE SECOND FLOOR - MED. SHOT
There is a small foyer here and doors leading into the courtroom. They come IN and go to the courtroom dor.
You all lift me up so I can see what's going on.
Jem and Scout make a pack saddle with their arms and Dill climbs up and peers in the glass at the top of the door.
Not much is happening. The Judge looks like he's sleeping. (MORE)
I see your daddy and a colored man. The colored man looks to me
like he's crying. Wonder what
Dill gets so absorbed in watching he stops talking. Scout and Jem begin to feel the strain of holding him up.
What's going on?
There are a lot of men sitting together on one side and one man
keeps pointing and yelling at the
colored man away.
Where is Atticus?
I can't see your daddy now, either. I wonder where in the
world -ANGLE WIDENS as behind them out of a side door COMES Atticus, FOLLOWED by Heck Tate. Atticus goes toward them, Heck Tate GOES on OUT of the foyer.
Scout. Jem. What in the world are you doing here?
They whirl around, dropping the startled Dill.
Oh, hello, Atticus.
We came down to find out where Boo Radley was locked up. We wanted to see the bats.
I want you both back home right away. Yes, sir.
ROBERT E. LEE EWELL, a short, bantam cock of a man, COMES INTO foreground. Mr. Finch.
Mr. Finch.
You run on, children. you at dinner. Yes, sir.

Howdy, Mr. Ewell.
Ewell seems nervous, ill-at-ease.
Atticus is very cold

Cap'n, I'm sorry they appointed you to defend that nigger that raped my Mayella. I don't know why I didn't just kill him instead of goin' for the Sheriff. Saved you and the Sheriff and the taxpayers a lot of trouble.
Mr. Ewell, if you'll excuse me, I'm very busy.

Cap'n, someone told me just now they thought you believed Tom Robinson's story agin ours. I said no, you're wrong there. Mr. Finch ain't takin' his story agin ours. Mr. Finch... (looks at Atticus who is eyeing him impassively They was wrong, wasn't they, Mr. Finch?
I've been appointed to defend Tom Robinson and now that he's been charged, that's what I intend doing.
You're takin' his story agin ours?
If you'll excuse me, Mr. Ewell.
Atticus starts on. Ewell blocks his way.
What kind of a man are you? got chullun of your own.

EXT. SIDEWALK IN FRONT OF FINCH HOUSE - NOON
Atticus comes from town. Miss Stephanie crosses from Miss Maudie's. They meet in front of his house. TWO SHOT - ATTICUS, MISS STEPHANIE
I hear John Taylor appointed you to defend that boy who raped Mayella Ewell.
Did he rape her?

BACK TO ATTICUS - As he talks he looks into the eyes of the men of the jury as if to find one to encourage him.

To begin with, this case should never have come to trial. The State has not produced on iota of medical evidence to the effect that the crime Tom Robinson is charged with ever took place. It has relied instead upon the testimony of two witnesses whose evidence has not only been called into serious question on cross examination, but has been flatly contradicted by the defendant. There is circumstantial evidence to indicate that Mayella Ewell was beaten savagely by someone who led almost exclusively with his left... and Tom Robinson now sits before you with the only good hand he possesses... his right hand. (MORE)

I have nothing but pity in my heart for the chief witness for the state. She is the victim of cruel poverty and ignorance -- but my pity does not extend so far as to her putting a man's life at stake which she has done in an effort to get rid of her own guilt. I say guilt, gentlemen, because it was guilt that motivated her. She has committed no crime, she has merely broken a rigid and time honored code of our society, a code so severe that whoever breaks it is hounded from our midst as unfit to live with. She must destroy the evidence of her offense. What was the evidence of her offense? Tom Robinson, a human being. She must put Tom Robinson away from her. Tom Robinson was her daily reminder of what she did. What did she do? She tempted a Negro. She was white, and she tempted a Negro. She did something that in our society is unspeakable: She kissed a black man. Not an old Uncle, but a strong, young Negro man. No code mattered to her before she broke it, but it came crashing down on her afterwards. The witnesses for the state, with the exception of the Sheriff of Maycomb County, have presented themselves to you gentlemen, to this Court, in the cynical confidence that their testimony would not be doubted, confident that you gentlemen would go along with them on the assumption -- the evil assumption -- that all Negroes lie, that all Negroes are basically immoral beings, that all Negro men are not to be trusted around our women, an assumption one associates with minds of their caliber, which, gentlemen, we know is in itself a lie I do not have to point out to you. And so a quiet, respectable, humble Negro who had the unmitigated temerity to "feel sorry" for a white woman has had to put his word against two white people's. (MORE)
The defendant is not guilty, but somebody in this courtroom is.

In this country our courts are the great levelers, and in our courts all men are created equal. I'm no idealist to believe firmly in the integrity of our courts and in the jury system. That is no ideal to me...It is a living, working reality. Gentlemen, a court is no better than each man of you sitting before me on this jury. A court is only as sound as the men who make it up. I am confident that you gentlemen will review without passion the evidence you have heard, come to a decision, and restore this defendant to his family. In the name of God, do your duty. In the name of God, believe Tom Robinson.

Well, the summer that had begun so long ago had ended, and another summer had taken its place, and a fall, and Boo Radley had come out and Jem was to live. I was to think of these days many times, of Jem, and Dill and Boo Radley and Tom Robinson and the Ewells and Atticus -- his fairness, his stubbornness, his devotion, his courage, his love.
